segments.title: Cruiser aviation as reconnaissance doctrine
segments.summary: The lecture compares why interwar navies put aircraft on cruisers, centering reconnaissance, force multiplication, scouting, sea control, and imperial presence.
segments.body: Sweden and France used aircraft to extend limited cruiser presence, Japan and the United States tied cruiser scouts to Pacific attrition and battle-fleet discovery, and the Royal Navy treated floatplanes as a sea-control tool for dispersed global cruisers.

evidence.note: The opening defines the episode as a summary of cruiser aviation in the major navies, with minor navies included for contrast.
The Swedish section introduces the recurring reconnaissance and information-gathering role, treating Gotland as an information hub and useful fleet asset.
The French section links cruiser aircraft to policing, reconnaissance, imperial presence, force maximization, and deterrent image.
The Japanese section explains why cruiser-launched reconnaissance protected carrier striking power and supported attrition doctrine.
The American section ties cruiser aircraft to the US Navy scouting force, finding the enemy, and enabling the battle force to act.
The Royal Navy section frames cruiser aviation around sea control, counter-raider work, and low- to medium-threat reconnaissance away from carrier support.
The conclusion describes the video as an introduction to cruiser reconnaissance and the doctrines behind it.
